content:
6 min read
Just now

--

If you are running a Model Context Protocol server in production, an important TypeScript SDK change is approaching.
The stable version of MCP TypeScript SDK v2 is expected to launch on July 28, 2026, alongside the final 2026-07-28 MCP specification. The new SDK is currently available in beta, while the v1 SDK remains the recommended version for production.
July 28 is not a forced migration deadline. Your existing MCP server will not suddenly stop working that day.
However, it is still an important date.
The official maintainers plan to move development toward v2 after the stable release. Version 1 will continue receiving bug fixes and security updates for at least six months, giving teams time to migrate safely.
The real problem is that this migration is not a normal dependency update.
You cannot simply change one version number, run npm install, and expect your server to work. The package structure, transports, schemas, handlers, runtime requirements, and protocol behavior are changing.
Teams operating MCP servers should start testing now, even if they keep v1 in production until v2 becomes stable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Your dependency tools may not find v2
The current TypeScript SDK is installed through one main package:
npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdkVersion 2 replaces this monolithic package with several smaller packages, including:
@modelcontextprotocol/server
@modelcontextprotocol/client
@modelcontextprotocol/core
@modelcontextprotocol/node
@modelcontextprotocol/expressThis package split is useful because applications only install the parts they need. A server project no longer needs to carry every client feature, transport, and runtime adapter.
However, it also creates a discovery problem.
Tools such as Dependabot or Renovate usually look for newer versions of packages already listed in your package.json. Since v2 uses different package names, your update tools may continue reporting that your v1 dependency is current.
In other words, your project may never receive a simple pull request saying, “MCP SDK v2 is available.”
Someone on your team must know the migration exists and plan it manually. The official migration guide confirms that v2 moves from @modelcontextprotocol/sdk to separate server, client, core, and runtime packages.
Node.js 20 is now required
Before changing any MCP code, check your production runtime.
MCP TypeScript SDK v2 requires Node.js 20 or newer. The SDK is ESM-first, but it also provides CommonJS builds, so both import and require() can still work.
This requirement may affect servers running on older Docker images, legacy virtual machines, serverless platforms, or company-managed environments.
Check the following places:

• Local development environments

• CI and deployment pipelines

• Docker base images

• Production servers

• Cloud runtime settings

• Monorepo package requirements
A migration can appear successful on a developer’s laptop while failing during deployment because production still uses Node.js 18.
Server-side SSE transport is removed
One of the most important changes affects remote MCP servers.
The old SSEServerTransport is removed from the main v2 server package. Developers are expected to migrate remote servers to Streamable HTTP.
A frozen legacy SSE package is available as a temporary bridge:
@modelcontextprotocol/server-legacy/sseHowever, it should not be treated as the long-term solution. It exists to give teams additional migration time.
WebSocket client transport is also removed because WebSocket is not an official MCP transport. Remote clients should generally use Streamable HTTP, while local process-based integrations can continue using stdio.
Teams should review their transport layer early because this part of the migration may require architecture changes, load-balancer updates, endpoint changes, and new integration tests.
Runtime-specific transports require a decision
The v2 SDK makes a clearer difference between Node.js transports and web-standard transports.
A traditional Node.js or Express server may use:
import { NodeStreamableHTTPServerTransport }
from "@modelcontextprotocol/node";A Cloudflare Worker, Deno application, Bun application, or Fetch API environment may instead use:
import { WebStandardStreamableHTTPServerTransport }
from "@modelcontextprotocol/server";The official codemod cannot always know where your code will run. It may choose the Node transport by default, leaving you to correct the import for web-standard environments.
This matters for libraries that support several runtimes. A shared package may need separate adapters instead of one universal server entry point.
Tool schemas are changing
The v2 SDK uses complete Standard Schema objects for tool inputs and outputs.
Older MCP server code may define a tool like this:
server.tool(
"get-weather",
{
city: z.string()
},
handler
);The newer registration style uses registerTool() and wraps the input in a complete schema:
server.registerTool(
"get-weather",
{
inputSchema: z.object({
city: z.string()
})
},
handler
);Standard Schema support means developers are not limited to one validation library. The SDK can work with compatible libraries such as Zod, Valibot, and ArkType.
For Zod users, the official migration guide recommends moving to Zod 4.2 or newer during the v2 migration.
Do not only test whether the server starts.
Test tools/list, tool calls, invalid inputs, structured outputs, and schema generation. Some dependency problems may only appear when a client asks the server to describe its tools.
Request handler registration also changes
Low-level MCP servers will see another breaking change.
In v1, a request handler may use a schema object:
server.setRequestHandler(
CallToolRequestSchema,
handler
);In v2, the handler uses a protocol method string:
server.setRequestHandler(
"tools/call",
handler
);The official codemod can update many common request handlers automatically. It also renames older methods such as .tool(), .prompt(), and .resource() to registerTool(), registerPrompt(), and registerResource().
But automated migration has limits.
For example, the codemod may not understand code where an MCP server object is passed through dependency injection, wrapped inside a custom class, or exposed through a library’s public API.
These cases need manual review and runtime testing.
The official codemod should be your starting point
The maintainers provide an official migration codemod:
npx @modelcontextprotocol/codemod@beta v1-to-v2 .Run it from the package root, not only inside the src folder. MCP imports may also exist in tests, scripts, examples, and fixtures. The tool can also update the nearest package.json.
Run the migration on a clean Git branch because the codemod changes files directly.
After it finishes, search for migration warnings:
grep -rn '@mcp-codemod-error' .These comments mark code that the tool understood but could not safely rewrite. Removed transports, unclear signatures, and features without direct replacements may require developer decisions.
Then run:
npm run typecheck
npm testAlso search the full repository for the old package name:
grep -rn "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk" .This can find old imports in CI scripts, dependency rules, test fixtures, documentation examples, or workspace packages.
SDK v2 does not automatically enable the new protocol
This is one of the easiest details to miss.
Upgrading your packages to v2 does not automatically make your client or server use the 2026-07-28 protocol revision.
By default, a manually created v2 client can continue using the older handshake and protocol behavior. Supporting the new protocol is an explicit decision.
A client can enable automatic protocol negotiation:
const client = new Client(
{
name: "my-client",
version: "1.0.0"
},
{
versionNegotiation: {
mode: "auto"
}
}
);Automatic mode checks whether the server supports the modern protocol and can fall back to the older protocol when needed.
This separation is important:
The SDK version is the library installed in your application. The protocol version is the language your MCP client and server use to communicate.
They are related, but they are not the same setting.
What teams should do before July 28
Do not replace a stable production MCP server with a beta SDK without testing.
Instead, create a migration branch and use the remaining time to identify problems.
A practical plan looks like this:

• Confirm that every environment supports Node.js 20.

• Run the official codemod on a clean branch.

• Replace old package imports with the correct v2 packages.

• Upgrade Zod and test generated tool schemas.

• Move server-side SSE endpoints to Streamable HTTP.

• Select the correct transport for Node or web-standard runtimes.

• Review low-level handlers and custom SDK wrappers.

• Test both old and new protocol negotiation.

• Deploy the migrated server to staging.

• Keep a simple rollback path to the v1 deployment.
The stable release date should be the start of a controlled production rollout, not the first day your team reads the migration guide.
Final thoughts
MCP TypeScript SDK v2 is not only a cleaner version of the existing package.
It changes how the SDK is installed, how servers register capabilities, how schemas are defined, how remote transports work, and how applications adopt the next MCP protocol revision.
The good news is that v1 will continue receiving support after v2 launches. You do not need to rush an untested beta into production.
But doing nothing also carries risk.
The best approach is simple: keep v1 stable, test v2 now, fix the hard parts before July 28, and move production only when the stable SDK and your own server are ready.
